## [00:00 - 03:04]

Okay. Hello, everyone. It is Monday, July 22nd, and we are going to play 321. It is a session offered to people in our story-worthy community, our VIP program. Join me once a month at least. I might do this more than once because I have so much fun doing it. My children love doing it. My students love doing it. My wife likes doing it. She's not as huge a fan as I am. She likes to listen, not participate as much. It's a game that I invented for workshops and it has two purposes. If you're not familiar with 321, the two purposes are one, we're going to look for new stories. I think that every time we find a new story, we find some gold. We find something that we can share with other people, both to improve our connections and trustworthiness and affection with other people. But also, if you're a business person, these stories can be invaluable. Also, most importantly, probably, the most important person we tell any story to is ourselves. Oftentimes, when we find a story from our past, it can explain the current state that we're in, which is enormously helpful. The first goal is to find those stories for all those reasons. The second is to practice strategies to be able to tell a story well in an impromptu way. Most of the stories we would tell, I would venture to say 95 to 100 percent of the stories that we tell are unprepared. We don't prepare stories for a dinner party or for a car ride or for a plane ride. Wherever we're telling stories to folks, we don't prepare stories ahead of time. We tend to have things happen to us and then we come home or we go to our friend's house. As I did today, I played golf with my buddies and I shared some stories. I didn't prep them ahead of time. The better we get at deploying strategies and storytelling in an automatic way, the same way in basketball, shooting a free throw becomes rather automatic for players after a period of time. Whereas if you're learning how to shoot a free throw, it requires a lot of concentration and thought about the particular moves. Actually, a better example is golf for me, at least. I used to have to think a lot before I hit my seven-eye or now I can just swing and all those things are automatic for me. We're going to make storytelling that for people too. It's also fun. If you're not familiar with 321, the numbers each stand for one part of the game. The three stands for three prompts. We're going to be receiving three prompts. I'm going to be using a website today, but you can also use the people in your life to provide the prompts or objects in the room, actually. I'm in this room a lot, so there are no sort of fresh objects for me, really. So I'm going to show you the website that I love to use.
